facts.text: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (1966) was filmed almost entirely in Spain, with the desert landscapes of Almería standing in for the American Southwest.
Clint Eastwood's iconic Man with No Name character in the Dollars Trilogy was inspired by Akira Kurosawa's samurai film Yojimbo (1961).
John Wayne appeared in over 170 films during his career, with the western genre making up the bulk of his legendary filmography.
Sergio Leone essentially invented the spaghetti western subgenre, directing landmark films that redefined how westerns were made.
Ennio Morricone composed over 500 film scores in his career, but is best remembered for his iconic spaghetti western soundtracks.
The Searchers (1956), directed by John Ford and starring John Wayne, is widely considered one of the greatest westerns ever made.
Shane (1953) ends with the famous line 'Shane, come back!' shouted by young Brandon deWilde as the mysterious gunfighter rides away.
High Noon (1952) unfolds in near real time, with the clock ticking toward the noon showdown forming the backbone of the film's tension.
Stagecoach (1939) was the film that transformed John Wayne from a B-movie actor into a major Hollywood star.
True Grit has been filmed twice — first in 1969 with John Wayne winning an Oscar, and again in 2010 by the Coen Brothers with Jeff Bridges.
Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid (1969) was one of the first buddy westerns, pairing Paul Newman and Robert Redford to enormous box office success.
The Magnificent Seven (1960) was a western remake of Akira Kurosawa's Seven Samurai (1954), transplanting the story from feudal Japan to Mexico.
A Fistful of Dollars (1964) was Clint Eastwood's first collaboration with Sergio Leone and launched the Dollars Trilogy.
Unforgiven (1992) was Clint Eastwood's revisionist take on the western genre, winning four Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director.
The Outlaw Josey Wales (1976) was directed by and starred Clint Eastwood as a Missouri farmer turned Confederate guerrilla seeking revenge.
Pale Rider (1985) features Eastwood as a mysterious preacher who helps gold miners stand up to a ruthless mining company.
No Country for Old Men (2007) won four Academy Awards, including Best Picture, blending western and thriller genres in a stark Texas landscape.
Django Unchained (2012) was Quentin Tarantino's tribute to spaghetti westerns, earning Jamie Foxx and Christoph Waltz widespread acclaim.
The Revenant (2015) finally won Leonardo DiCaprio his first Academy Award for Best Actor after multiple previous nominations.
Hell or High Water (2016) was a modern-day western set in West Texas, earning four Academy Award nominations including Best Picture.
Tombstone (1993) features Val Kilmer's iconic portrayal of Doc Holliday, often cited as one of the best supporting performances in any western.
Yellowstone, created by Taylor Sheridan, premiered in 2018 and became the most-watched cable television series in the United States.
Gunsmoke ran for 20 seasons from 1955 to 1975, making it one of the longest-running primetime live-action series in American TV history.
Bonanza aired from 1959 to 1973 and was the first regularly scheduled TV program broadcast in color on NBC.
Deadwood (2004–2006) was an HBO series set in the lawless South Dakota mining camp of the 1870s, known for its Shakespearean dialogue.
Westworld (2016–2022) reimagined the western as a futuristic AI-driven theme park, blending sci-fi and western genres.
Longmire (2012–2017) was based on Craig Johnson's Walt Longmire mystery novels set in fictional Absaroka County, Wyoming.
John Ford directed more classic westerns than any other filmmaker, including Stagecoach, The Searchers, and The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance.
Sam Peckinpah earned the nickname 'Bloody Sam' for his graphic depictions of violence in westerns like The Wild Bunch (1969).
Sergio Leone's Once Upon a Time in the West (1968) is considered by many critics to be the greatest spaghetti western ever made.
Howard Hawks directed Red River (1948) and Rio Bravo (1959), two of John Wayne's most celebrated westerns.
Wyatt Earp was a real-life lawman best known for the Gunfight at the O.K. Corral in Tombstone, Arizona, in 1881.
Billy the Kid was allegedly killed by Sheriff Pat Garrett on July 14, 1881, at the age of 21, though some legends claim he survived.
Doc Holliday was a dentist turned gambler and gunfighter who became Wyatt Earp's closest ally at the O.K. Corral.
Jesse James was one of the most notorious outlaws in American history, leading a gang of bank and train robbers in the post-Civil War era.
Geronimo was an Apache leader who fought against both Mexican and American expansion into Apache tribal lands for over 25 years.
Monument Valley, straddling the Arizona-Utah border, became the iconic western filming location thanks to John Ford's frequent use of it.
The town of Lone Pine, California, has served as a filming location for hundreds of westerns since the silent film era.
Old Tucson Studios in Arizona was built for the 1939 film Arizona and has since been used in over 400 film and TV productions.
Almería, Spain, became the go-to filming location for spaghetti westerns in the 1960s due to its arid, desert-like terrain.
The western genre's iconic showdown — two gunslingers facing off on a dusty street — was largely popularized by High Noon (1952).
The 'white hat vs. black hat' trope in westerns originated from early silent films where heroes wore white and villains wore black.
Saloon brawls became a staple of the western genre, with breakaway furniture specially designed for stunt scenes.
The tumbling tumbleweed, an iconic symbol of western films, is actually an invasive species originally from Russia.
Dances with Wolves (1990) won seven Academy Awards, including Best Picture, and revitalized the western genre after years of decline.
Cimarron (1931) was the first western to win the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Unforgiven (1992) and No Country for Old Men (2007) are the only westerns to win Best Picture at the Oscars since Dances with Wolves.
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly grossed over $25 million at the box office on a budget of just $1.2 million.
Kevin Costner both directed and starred in Dances with Wolves, a risky move that paid off with massive critical and commercial success.
The Wild Bunch (1969) was highly controversial upon release for its graphic violence but is now considered a masterpiece of the genre.
Once Upon a Time in the West features one of cinema's longest opening sequences — nearly 15 minutes with almost no dialogue.
Blazing Saddles (1974), directed by Mel Brooks, is one of the most famous western comedies, satirizing racism and genre conventions.
The Dollars Trilogy — A Fistful of Dollars, For a Few Dollars More, and The Good, the Bad and the Ugly — made Clint Eastwood an international star.
Lee Van Cleef went from bit parts in Hollywood to stardom in spaghetti westerns, becoming known as one of the great on-screen villains.
Eli Wallach's portrayal of Tuco in The Good, the Bad and the Ugly is considered one of the most memorable characters in western cinema.
John Wayne won his only Academy Award for Best Actor for his role as Rooster Cogburn in True Grit (1969).
Gary Cooper won the Academy Award for Best Actor for High Noon at the 25th Academy Awards ceremony in 1953.
The spaghetti western got its name because these films were produced by Italian studios, despite being set in the American West.
Over 600 spaghetti westerns were made between 1964 and 1978, mostly shot in studios in Rome and on location in Spain.
Ennio Morricone's score for The Good, the Bad and the Ugly, featuring the iconic 'wah wah wah' melody, is one of the most recognizable film scores ever composed.
The ecstasy of gold sequence in The Good, the Bad and the Ugly — Tuco running through a cemetery — is one of cinema's greatest scenes set to music.
Charles Bronson starred in Once Upon a Time in the West after Eastwood turned down the role of Harmonica.
Henry Fonda played against type as the villain Frank in Once Upon a Time in the West, shocking audiences who knew him as a hero.
The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962) explores the tension between myth and reality with the line 'When the legend becomes fact, print the legend.'
My Darling Clementine (1946), directed by John Ford, tells a fictionalized version of the events leading to the Gunfight at the O.K. Corral.
Red River (1948) features one of John Wayne's most complex performances as a tyrannical cattle drive boss.
Rio Bravo (1959) was Howard Hawks' response to High Noon, which he felt portrayed a marshal as too weak and dependent on public help.
Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid was the highest-grossing film of 1969, earning over $102 million domestically.
The real Butch Cassidy (Robert LeRoy Parker) and the Sundance Kid (Harry Longabaugh) allegedly died in a shootout in Bolivia in 1908.
Tombstone (1993) was originally to be directed by Kevin Jarre, who was fired during production and replaced by George P. Cosmatos.
Val Kilmer reportedly studied historical accounts of Doc Holliday extensively to prepare for his role in Tombstone.
The line 'I'm your huckleberry' from Tombstone became one of the most quoted lines in modern western cinema.
Wyatt Earp (1994), starring Kevin Costner, was released just six months after Tombstone and was far less successful at the box office.
3:10 to Yuma has been filmed twice — in 1957 with Glenn Ford and in 2007 with Russell Crowe and Christian Bale.
Open Range (2003), directed by and starring Kevin Costner, was praised for one of the most realistic gunfight sequences in western film history.
Appaloosa (2008) starred Ed Harris and Viggo Mortensen as lawmen hired to tame a violent rancher played by Jeremy Irons.
The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford (2007) starred Brad Pitt and is known for its lyrical, contemplative style.
True Grit (2010) by the Coen Brothers earned ten Academy Award nominations but won none.
Jeff Bridges earned a Best Actor nomination for his role as Rooster Cogburn in the Coen Brothers' True Grit.
Hailee Steinfeld made her film debut in True Grit (2010) at age 14, earning a Best Supporting Actress nomination.
Django Unchained earned over $425 million worldwide, making it Quentin Tarantino's highest-grossing film at the time.
Christoph Waltz won his second Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for Django Unchained, having previously won for Inglourious Basterds.
The Hateful Eight (2015) was Tarantino's second western, filmed in Ultra Panavision 70, a format not used since 1966.
The Revenant was filmed using only natural light, with cinematographer Emmanuel Lubezki winning his third consecutive Oscar for the film.
The Revenant is based on the true story of frontiersman Hugh Glass, who was mauled by a grizzly bear and left for dead in 1823.
Hell or High Water was written by Taylor Sheridan, who also wrote Sicario and Wind River, forming an unofficial frontier trilogy.
Wind River (2017), directed by Taylor Sheridan, is set on the Wind River Indian Reservation in Wyoming and explores crimes against Native Americans.
Hostiles (2017) starred Christian Bale as a U.S. Army captain escorting a Cheyenne war chief and his family back to their homeland.
The Power of the Dog (2021), directed by Jane Campion, was a psychological western that earned twelve Academy Award nominations.
Jane Campion won the Academy Award for Best Director for The Power of the Dog, becoming only the third woman to win the award.
News of the World (2020) starred Tom Hanks as a traveling newsreader who agrees to escort an orphaned girl across Texas.
The Harder They Fall (2021) featured an entirely Black principal cast, drawing on real historical figures from the Old West.
Old Henry (2021) was a low-budget western that received strong critical praise for Tim Blake Nelson's lead performance.
Bone Tomahawk (2015) blended horror and western genres, with Kurt Russell leading a rescue party into cannibal territory.
Slow West (2015) was a stylish, offbeat western starring Michael Fassbender, set in 1870s Colorado.
The Sisters Brothers (2018), starring Joaquin Phoenix and John C. Reilly, was based on Patrick deWitt's acclaimed novel.
Yellowstone became a cultural phenomenon, spawning prequel series 1883 and 1923.
1883 follows the Dutton family's original journey from Tennessee to Montana, starring Sam Elliott, Tim McGraw, and Faith Hill.
1923 stars Harrison Ford and Helen Mirren as earlier generation Duttons facing Prohibition, drought, and the Great Depression.
Kevin Costner departed Yellowstone after Season 5 due to scheduling conflicts with his own western film project, Horizon.
Deadwood won eight Primetime Emmy Awards during its three-season run and was followed by a 2019 TV movie.
Ian McShane's portrayal of Al Swearengen in Deadwood is considered one of the greatest performances in television history.
Westworld was based on Michael Crichton's 1973 film of the same name, in which robots in a western theme park malfunction.
Gunsmoke holds the record for the most episodes of any prime-time live-action drama in American television history, with 635 episodes.
James Arness starred as Marshal Matt Dillon in Gunsmoke for all 20 seasons.
Bonanza made television history as one of the first shows to deal with social issues like racism and land rights within a western format.
The Lone Ranger radio show debuted in 1933 and spawned a TV series (1949–1957) and multiple films.
Rawhide (1959–1965) starred a young Clint Eastwood as Rowdy Yates before he became a film star in spaghetti westerns.
The Rifleman (1958–1963) starred Chuck Connors as a widowed rancher in the New Mexico Territory known for his modified Winchester rifle.
Have Gun – Will Travel (1957–1963) starred Richard Boone as Paladin, a refined gunfighter-for-hire based in San Francisco.
Maverick (1957–1962) starred James Garner as a charming, wisecracking gambler, introducing humor into the TV western genre.
The Virginian (1962–1971) was the first 90-minute western series on American television.
Hell on Wheels (2011–2016) followed the construction of the First Transcontinental Railroad in the aftermath of the Civil War.
Justified (2010–2015) starred Timothy Olyphant as Deputy U.S. Marshal Raylan Givens, blending modern crime drama with western sensibilities.
Godless (2017) was a Netflix limited series set in a 1880s mining town populated almost entirely by women.
The Son (2017–2019) starred Pierce Brosnan in an AMC series spanning 150 years of a Texas ranching family's history.
Walker, Texas Ranger (1993–2001) starred Chuck Norris and blended martial arts with traditional western lawman tropes.
Sergio Leone directed only seven films in his career, but his westerns fundamentally changed the genre forever.
Sergio Corbucci directed Django (1966), a spaghetti western so violent it was banned in several countries.
The original Django (1966) inspired over 30 unofficial sequels and heavily influenced Quentin Tarantino's Django Unchained.
Anthony Mann directed a series of acclaimed westerns with James Stewart in the 1950s, including Winchester '73, Bend of the River, and The Man from Laramie.
Budd Boetticher directed a cycle of lean, taut westerns with Randolph Scott in the late 1950s known as the Ranown Cycle.
Clint Eastwood directed eleven westerns over his career, more than any other major actor-director of his generation.
Kevin Costner's Horizon: An American Saga was envisioned as a four-part epic exploring westward expansion over 15 years.
George Stevens' Shane is known for its breathtaking Grand Teton mountain backdrop filmed in Jackson Hole, Wyoming.
Fred Zinnemann directed High Noon, which was also an allegory for the Hollywood blacklist during the McCarthy era.
John Sturges directed both The Magnificent Seven (1960) and Gunfight at the O.K. Corral (1957).
Quentin Tarantino's westerns are noted for blending revisionist history with exploitation film aesthetics and sharp dialogue.
The Coen Brothers directed both True Grit (2010) and No Country for Old Men (2007), two of the most acclaimed modern westerns.
Taylor Sheridan grew up on a ranch in Texas, which deeply influenced his western-themed writing and directing.
Wild Bill Hickok was a real gunfighter and lawman who was shot dead while playing poker in Deadwood, South Dakota, in 1876.
The hand Hickok was holding when he died — aces and eights — became known as the 'dead man's hand' in poker.
Calamity Jane was a frontierswoman known for her sharpshooting and her association with Wild Bill Hickok.
Judge Roy Bean was a real-life eccentric justice of the peace in West Texas who called himself 'the Law West of the Pecos.'
Annie Oakley was a sharpshooter who starred in Buffalo Bill's Wild West show and could split a playing card edge-on from 30 paces.
Buffalo Bill Cody's Wild West show toured the United States and Europe from 1883 to 1913, mythologizing the American frontier.
Sitting Bull briefly toured with Buffalo Bill's Wild West show in 1885 before returning to the Standing Rock Reservation.
The Gunfight at the O.K. Corral lasted only about 30 seconds but has been depicted in dozens of films and TV shows.
Cochise was an Apache chief who led resistance against American and Mexican forces in the 1860s and was portrayed in the 1950 film Broken Arrow.
Crazy Horse was an Oglala Lakota warrior who helped defeat Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in 1876.
Bat Masterson was a lawman, gambler, and journalist who served as sheriff of Ford County, Kansas, and later became a sports writer in New York.
Butch Cassidy led the Wild Bunch gang, which carried out the longest string of successful bank and train robberies in American history.
The James-Younger Gang, led by Jesse and Frank James, robbed banks, stagecoaches, and trains across the Midwest in the 1860s and 1870s.
Jesse James was shot and killed by Robert Ford, a member of his own gang, on April 3, 1882, for a $10,000 reward.
Pat Garrett wrote a book about killing Billy the Kid titled 'The Authentic Life of Billy the Kid' shortly after the shooting.
Geronimo surrendered for the final time on September 4, 1886, making him the last major Native American leader to formally surrender to the U.S. military.
Bass Reeves was one of the first Black deputy U.S. marshals west of the Mississippi, arresting over 3,000 felons in his career.
The real Lone Ranger may have been inspired by Bass Reeves, though this connection is debated by historians.
Monument Valley's iconic buttes — including the Mittens and Merrick Butte — have appeared in at least ten John Ford films.
Durango, Mexico, became a popular filming location for westerns in the 1950s and 1960s, hosting productions starring John Wayne and others.
Cinecittà Studios in Rome was used for interior scenes in many spaghetti westerns during the 1960s.
The Tabernas Desert in Almería, Spain, still has standing western film sets that are now tourist attractions.
Kanab, Utah, was nicknamed 'Little Hollywood' because so many westerns were filmed there from the 1920s through the 1970s.
Texas Ranch in the Tabernas Desert served as the set for The Good, the Bad and the Ugly and many other spaghetti westerns.
Johnson Canyon in Alberta, Canada, was used as a filming location for Unforgiven (1992).
The Sonoran Desert in Arizona provided the backdrop for numerous westerns including Tombstone and Arizona (1940).
Moab, Utah, has served as a filming location for westerns since the 1940s, including Rio Grande (1950) and Westworld.
Paramount Ranch in the Santa Monica Mountains served as a western film set from the 1920s until it was destroyed by the Woolsey Fire in 2018.
The classic western showdown at high noon was a narrative device that built tension by giving the audience a defined deadline.
The lone gunslinger archetype in westerns represents rugged individualism and the American ideal of self-reliance.
Western movies often feature the tension between civilization and wilderness, with the frontier serving as a boundary between order and chaos.
The 'cavalry to the rescue' trope originated in early westerns where the U.S. Cavalry arrives just in time to save settlers.
Revisionist westerns challenge traditional genre tropes by portraying Native Americans more sympathetically and questioning frontier mythology.
The anti-hero became central to the western genre in the 1960s, replacing the clean-cut heroism of earlier films.
Western film villains often wear black clothing, a visual shorthand that dates back to the silent film era.
The quick-draw duel is largely a Hollywood invention — real gunfights in the Old West were usually messy and chaotic.
Spurs, cowboy hats, and leather chaps became iconic western imagery largely because of their prominence in early Hollywood westerns.
The Winchester rifle is one of the most iconic weapons in western films, often called 'the gun that won the West.'
The Colt Single Action Army revolver, also known as the Peacemaker, is the most recognizable handgun in western cinema.
Horse stunts in early westerns were often dangerous, leading to the formation of the American Humane Association's film monitoring program.
The phrase 'This town ain't big enough for the both of us' became a quintessential western cliché despite rarely appearing in actual films.
Western film scores often feature whistling, harmonicas, and twangy guitars to evoke the loneliness of the frontier.
The poncho worn by Clint Eastwood's Man with No Name was purchased at a thrift store in Santa Monica before filming A Fistful of Dollars.
Dances with Wolves was one of the first major Hollywood films to feature extensive dialogue in the Lakota Sioux language.
The Magnificent Seven (1960) featured an ensemble cast including Yul Brynner, Steve McQueen, Charles Bronson, and James Coburn.
Steve McQueen famously tried to upstage Yul Brynner in The Magnificent Seven by performing distracting bits of business during Brynner's scenes.
The Magnificent Seven was remade in 2016 with Denzel Washington, Chris Pratt, and Ethan Hawke.
For a Few Dollars More (1965) introduced Lee Van Cleef as a bounty hunter partnering with Eastwood's Man with No Name.
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly features a climactic three-way Mexican standoff in a circular cemetery, one of cinema's most iconic scenes.
Ennio Morricone used gunshots, cracking whips, and electric guitars in his western scores, breaking from traditional orchestral film music.
Morricone's score for Once Upon a Time in the West was composed before filming began, and Leone directed scenes to match the music.
The Dollars Trilogy was not originally intended as a trilogy — the three films share no continuous storyline.
Clint Eastwood was paid $15,000 for A Fistful of Dollars, a modest sum that grew exponentially as the film became a worldwide hit.
A Fistful of Dollars was an unauthorized remake of Yojimbo, leading to a lawsuit by Kurosawa's production company that was settled out of court.
Sergio Leone wanted to cast Henry Fonda as the villain in A Fistful of Dollars, but Fonda was unavailable until Once Upon a Time in the West.
The climactic duel in The Good, the Bad and the Ugly lasts nearly five minutes, with most of that time spent on close-ups of the three men's eyes.
Duck, You Sucker! (1971), also known as A Fistful of Dynamite, was Sergio Leone's last western.
John Wayne's real name was Marion Robert Morrison, which he changed early in his career.
John Wayne starred in his first major western, The Big Trail (1930), at just 23 years old, but the film flopped.
John Ford gave Wayne his breakout role in Stagecoach after the actor spent nearly a decade in B-westerns.
The Shootist (1976) was John Wayne's final film, in which he played a dying gunfighter — Wayne himself was battling cancer at the time.
John Wayne and director John Ford collaborated on over 20 films, forming one of Hollywood's most legendary partnerships.
She Wore a Yellow Ribbon (1949) features some of John Ford's most stunning Technicolor photography of Monument Valley.
The Horse Soldiers (1959) starred John Wayne and William Holden in a Civil War-era western based on a real Union cavalry raid.
El Dorado (1966) was essentially a reworking of Rio Bravo, with John Wayne again playing a lawman needing help from unlikely allies.
John Wayne received his only Academy Award for True Grit, playing the one-eyed, hard-drinking Marshal Rooster Cogburn.
Wayne's eyepatch in True Grit was worn on his left eye, and he performed many of his own stunts despite being 62 years old.
The Cowboys (1972) controversially featured John Wayne's character being killed on screen, a first for the actor.
Clint Eastwood served in the U.S. Army before becoming an actor, stationed at Fort Ord in California during the Korean War.
Eastwood got his start on the TV western Rawhide before traveling to Italy to make A Fistful of Dollars.
High Plains Drifter (1973) was Eastwood's first western as director, featuring a mysterious stranger who may be a ghost.
The Outlaw Josey Wales featured Chief Dan George in a memorable role as a Cherokee elder, earning widespread praise.
Eastwood dedicated Unforgiven to directors Sergio Leone and Don Siegel, his two greatest mentors.
Unforgiven was Eastwood's statement that violence has real consequences, countering the glamorized gunplay of earlier westerns.
Eastwood kept the Unforgiven script by David Webb Peoples for nearly 20 years before deciding he was old enough to play the lead.
Gene Hackman won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for his role as the brutal Sheriff Little Bill Daggett in Unforgiven.
Pale Rider was the first western in years to reach number one at the box office when it was released in 1985.
Two Mules for Sister Sara (1970) paired Eastwood with Shirley MacLaine in a comedic western set during the Franco-Mexican War.
Hang 'Em High (1968) was Eastwood's first American-made western after the Dollars Trilogy, set in the Oklahoma Territory.
Joe Kidd (1972) was a lesser-known Eastwood western directed by John Sturges and set during the New Mexican land grant wars.
Bronco Billy (1980) was Eastwood's affectionate comedy about a modern-day cowboy running a traveling Wild West show.
Cry Macho (2021) was Eastwood's most recent film, making him the oldest director of a major studio release at 91.
The Ox-Bow Incident (1943) was an early anti-lynching western that challenged the genre's usual moral simplicity.
Johnny Guitar (1954) starred Joan Crawford in a highly unusual western that has been interpreted as a McCarthy-era allegory.
The Treasure of the Sierra Madre (1948), directed by John Huston, is a western-adjacent film about gold prospectors driven mad by greed.
Butch Cassidy's Hole-in-the-Wall hideout was a real remote valley in northern Wyoming used by outlaws as a refuge.
The Sundance Kid got his nickname from the town of Sundance, Wyoming, where he was once jailed for horse theft.
Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid features the famous cliff-jumping scene where Newman and Redford leap into a river to escape a posse.
The bicycle scene in Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid, set to 'Raindrops Keep Fallin' on My Head,' won an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
William Goldman wrote the screenplay for Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid, which sold for a then-record $400,000.
Little Big Man (1970) starred Dustin Hoffman as a 121-year-old man recounting his life among the Cheyenne and at Custer's Last Stand.
Jeremiah Johnson (1972) starred Robert Redford as a mountain man in the 1850s Rockies, filmed on location in Utah.
The Outlaw (1943), produced by Howard Hughes, was controversial for its emphasis on Jane Russell's physical appearance.
Cat Ballou (1965) won Lee Marvin the Academy Award for Best Actor for his dual role as a drunken gunfighter and his villainous twin.
McCabe & Mrs. Miller (1971), directed by Robert Altman, deconstructed western myths with a bleak, rain-soaked Pacific Northwest setting.
Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid (1973) was directed by Sam Peckinpah and featured Bob Dylan in an acting role and on the soundtrack.
The Ballad of Cable Hogue (1970) was Sam Peckinpah's most gentle western, a comedy-drama about a prospector finding water in the desert.
Silverado (1985), directed by Lawrence Kasdan, was an ensemble western starring Kevin Kline, Scott Glenn, Danny Glover, and Kevin Costner.
Young Guns (1988) featured the Brat Pack — Emilio Estevez, Kiefer Sutherland, Lou Diamond Phillips — reimagining Billy the Kid's story.
Tombstone's famous line 'I'll be your huckleberry' was likely a period expression meaning 'I'm the right person for the job.'
Kurt Russell reportedly ghost-directed much of Tombstone after Kevin Jarre was removed as director.
Powers Boothe played the villain Curly Bill Brocius in Tombstone and was praised for his menacing performance.
Sam Elliott played Virgil Earp in Tombstone, bringing his signature deep voice and mustache to the role.
The Quick and the Dead (1995) starred Sharon Stone, Gene Hackman, Russell Crowe, and a young Leonardo DiCaprio in a stylish gunfight tournament western.
Legends of the Fall (1994) starred Brad Pitt in an epic western drama set in early 20th century Montana.
Maverick (1994) starred Mel Gibson, Jodie Foster, and James Garner in a fun, lighthearted western based on the 1950s TV show.
City Slickers (1991) is a comedy western where Billy Crystal and friends go on a cattle drive led by Jack Palance's grizzled cowboy Curly.
Jack Palance won the Best Supporting Actor Oscar for City Slickers and famously did one-armed push-ups on stage.
Lonesome Dove (1989) is a beloved TV miniseries based on Larry McMurtry's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel about a cattle drive from Texas to Montana.
Robert Duvall and Tommy Lee Jones starred in Lonesome Dove, delivering performances widely regarded as among the best in TV western history.
Broken Trail (2006) was an AMC miniseries starring Robert Duvall as a cowboy rescuing Chinese girls from forced labor.
Dead Man (1995), directed by Jim Jarmusch, is a surreal, black-and-white western starring Johnny Depp with a score by Neil Young.
The Proposition (2005) is an Australian western set in the 1880s Outback, written by musician Nick Cave.
Meek's Cutoff (2010), directed by Kelly Reichardt, is a minimalist western about settlers lost on the Oregon Trail.
The Beguiled (1971 and 2017) is set during the Civil War at a Southern girls' school where an injured soldier disrupts the household.
Rango (2011) is an animated western comedy starring Johnny Depp as a chameleon who becomes the sheriff of a frontier town.
No Country for Old Men is based on Cormac McCarthy's 2005 novel of the same name, set along the Texas-Mexico border.
Javier Bardem's portrayal of Anton Chigurh in No Country for Old Men, with his cattle bolt weapon and bowl haircut, became instantly iconic.
Javier Bardem won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for No Country for Old Men.
The Coen Brothers filmed No Country for Old Men in New Mexico and West Texas, capturing the spare, menacing landscape.
Blood Meridian, another Cormac McCarthy western novel, has been called 'unfilmable' for decades due to its extreme violence.
All the Pretty Horses (2000), based on McCarthy's novel, starred Matt Damon in a coming-of-age western set in 1949 Texas and Mexico.
The Ballad of Buster Scruggs (2018) by the Coen Brothers is a western anthology film consisting of six distinct short stories.
Django Unchained's script by Tarantino was 166 pages long, unusually lengthy for a Hollywood screenplay.
Leonardo DiCaprio cut his hand on broken glass during the filming of Django Unchained and continued acting, incorporating the real blood into the scene.
Samuel L. Jackson's role as Stephen in Django Unchained was one of his most controversial, playing an enslaved man loyal to his enslaver.
Quentin Tarantino considers The Good, the Bad and the Ugly to be the greatest film ever made.
The Hateful Eight was presented as a 70mm roadshow with an overture and intermission, echoing the epic films of the 1960s.
The Revenant required a grueling 9-month shoot in remote locations in Canada and Argentina.
Tom Hardy played the antagonist John Fitzgerald in The Revenant, earning a Best Supporting Actor nomination.
Alejandro González Iñárritu won the Academy Award for Best Director for The Revenant, his second consecutive directing Oscar.
The bear attack scene in The Revenant took weeks to film and used a combination of CGI and practical effects.
Hell or High Water grossed $37.9 million domestically against a $12 million budget, becoming a sleeper hit.
Ben Foster and Chris Pine played bank-robbing brothers in Hell or High Water, with Jeff Bridges as the pursuing Texas Ranger.
Jeff Bridges earned his seventh Academy Award nomination for Hell or High Water.
The Magnificent Seven (2016) was directed by Antoine Fuqua and marked the first time a Black actor led a western ensemble since the genre's golden age.
Denzel Washington became only the second Black actor to lead a major studio western since Sidney Poitier in Buck and the Preacher (1972).
The western genre experienced its golden age in the 1940s and 1950s, when dozens of western films were released each year.
By the late 1960s, spaghetti westerns had largely displaced traditional Hollywood westerns in popularity.
The western genre nearly died out in the 1980s before being revived by films like Silverado and Pale Rider.
Dances with Wolves' Best Picture win in 1991 marked a major comeback for the western genre at the Academy Awards.
The 2000s and 2010s saw a resurgence of westerns, often labeled 'neo-westerns,' blending modern settings with frontier themes.
The first western film ever made is generally considered to be The Great Train Robbery (1903), directed by Edwin S. Porter.
The Great Train Robbery was only 12 minutes long but pioneered editing techniques and narrative storytelling in cinema.
Tom Mix was the biggest western star of the silent film era, appearing in 291 films between 1910 and 1935.
William S. Hart was one of the first major western film stars, known for bringing realism to the genre in the silent era.
Gene Autry was the original 'singing cowboy,' appearing in 93 feature films and owning the California Angels baseball team.
Roy Rogers, known as the 'King of the Cowboys,' appeared in over 100 films and was one of the most popular western stars of the 1940s and 1950s.
Hopalong Cassidy, played by William Boyd, was one of the first TV western heroes, with his show airing from 1952 to 1954.
The Lone Ranger TV series, starring Clayton Moore, ran from 1949 to 1957 and popularized the silver bullet and mask imagery.
Tonto, the Lone Ranger's companion, was played by Jay Silverheels, one of the first Native American actors to achieve mainstream fame.
The Cisco Kid was one of the first TV characters to be shown in color, with the series airing 156 episodes from 1950 to 1956.
Zorro is technically a western character — the masked swordsman first appeared in Johnston McCulley's 1919 story 'The Curse of Capistrano.'
The western genre has been adapted globally, with 'curry westerns' in India, 'kimchi westerns' in Korea, and 'osterns' in Eastern Europe.
Sukiyaki Western Django (2007) was a Japanese western directed by Takashi Miike, blending samurai and spaghetti western traditions.
El Topo (1970) by Alejandro Jodorowsky is a surrealist western that became one of the first midnight movies.
The Good, the Bad, the Weird (2008) is a South Korean western set in 1930s Manchuria, inspired by Leone's classic.
Australian westerns, sometimes called 'meat pie westerns,' include films like Quigley Down Under (1990) and The Proposition (2005).
Quigley Down Under (1990) starred Tom Selleck as an American sharpshooter hired to work in the Australian Outback.
The Dark Tower series by Stephen King blends western and fantasy genres, with a gunslinger as its central character.
Westworld (1973), the original film written and directed by Michael Crichton, was the first feature film to use digital image processing.
Back to the Future Part III (1990) is set in the Old West of 1885, making it a sci-fi western comedy.
Cowboys & Aliens (2011) blended the western and sci-fi genres, starring Daniel Craig and Harrison Ford.
Wild Wild West (1999) starred Will Smith in a steampunk western that was a major box office disappointment.
The Valley of Gwangi (1969) combined westerns with dinosaurs, featuring Ray Harryhausen's stop-motion visual effects.
Firefly (2002–2003), created by Joss Whedon, was a space western TV series that developed an enormous cult following despite being cancelled after one season.
The Mandalorian (2019–present) has been described as a space western, drawing heavily from samurai and spaghetti western traditions.
Westworld's first season on HBO was the most-watched debut season in HBO history at the time of its premiere.
Evan Rachel Wood and Thandiwe Newton received critical acclaim for their roles as sentient AI hosts in Westworld.
The western genre's decline in the 1970s coincided with the rise of the science fiction blockbuster, led by Star Wars (1977).
George Lucas drew heavily from western and samurai films when creating Star Wars, particularly from John Ford and Akira Kurosawa.
Many western tropes — the lone hero, the frontier town, the final showdown — were adapted directly into the sci-fi and superhero genres.
The Dollars Trilogy was shot using Techniscope, a cheaper widescreen process that gave spaghetti westerns their distinctive grainy look.
Sergio Leone pioneered the use of extreme close-ups of actors' eyes during standoff scenes, a technique widely imitated since.
Leone's pacing, characterized by long, tension-building silences, was the opposite of the fast-paced American westerns of the era.
Ennio Morricone and Sergio Leone were elementary school classmates in Rome before becoming collaborators.
Morricone received an honorary Academy Award in 2007 for his contributions to cinema before winning his first competitive Oscar for The Hateful Eight in 2016.
The score for The Hateful Eight was Ennio Morricone's first western score in over 40 years.
Dimitri Tiomkin composed the scores for High Noon, Gunfight at the O.K. Corral, and Rio Bravo.
The High Noon theme song 'Do Not Forsake Me, Oh My Darlin'' won the Academy Award for Best Original Song in 1953.
Elmer Bernstein's score for The Magnificent Seven is one of the most recognized film scores in history and has been used in countless commercials.
Jerome Moross composed the sweeping score for The Big Country (1958), which became synonymous with the epic western landscape.
Hans Zimmer composed the scores for several modern westerns, including Spirit: Stallion of the Cimarron and The Lone Ranger (2013).
Johnny Cash recorded several western-themed albums and appeared in western films and TV shows, including a guest role on Columbo.
Marty Robbins' 'El Paso' (1959) is one of the most famous western-themed songs in country music history.
Ghosts of the Abyss director James Cameron originally planned to make a western before pivoting to Titanic.
Stagecoach (1939) established many western conventions: the diverse group of strangers, the Native American attack, and the hero's redemption.
John Ford shot nine films in Monument Valley, including Stagecoach, She Wore a Yellow Ribbon, and The Searchers.
The Searchers features Ethan Edwards' famous doorway shot, framing John Wayne against the wilderness as a man who can never return to civilization.
Martin Scorsese has cited The Searchers as a major influence on Taxi Driver (1976).
Shane's final gunfight in the muddy saloon is considered one of the most perfectly staged action scenes in classic cinema.
Alan Ladd, who starred in Shane, stood only 5'6" tall — camera angles and platform shoes were used to make him appear taller.
High Noon was filmed in just 28 days on a tight budget, contributing to its tense, stripped-down aesthetic.
Grace Kelly made one of her early major film appearances as the Quaker wife of Gary Cooper's marshal in High Noon.
Stagecoach (1939) was nominated for seven Academy Awards and won two: Best Supporting Actor for Thomas Mitchell and Best Score.
The Alamo (1960) was directed by and starred John Wayne as Davy Crockett, and was nominated for seven Academy Awards.
How the West Was Won (1962) was filmed in three-panel Cinerama and featured an all-star cast including John Wayne, James Stewart, and Henry Fonda.
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly was actually the third film shot in the Dollars Trilogy but serves as a prequel set during the Civil War.
Clint Eastwood was not Sergio Leone's first choice for A Fistful of Dollars — James Coburn and Charles Bronson both turned down the role.
Lee Marvin starred in both Cat Ballou (1965) and Paint Your Wagon (1969), two of the rare western musicals.
Paint Your Wagon (1969) featured Clint Eastwood singing, a rare and often-mocked departure from his tough-guy persona.
The Professionals (1966) starred Burt Lancaster and Lee Marvin on a mission to rescue a kidnapped woman from a Mexican bandit.
Once Upon a Time in the West was a commercial failure in the United States upon release but became a massive hit in Europe.
The Wild Bunch (1969) was one of the first films to use slow-motion violence extensively, influencing action films for decades.
Sam Peckinpah's use of multi-angle editing in The Wild Bunch's final shootout required over 3,600 individual edits.
Bring Me the Head of Alfredo Garcia (1974) was Peckinpah's most personal film, set along the Texas-Mexico border.
The Missouri Breaks (1976) paired Marlon Brando and Jack Nicholson in a quirky, revisionist western about horse thieves and a hired killer.
Heaven's Gate (1980), directed by Michael Cimino, was such a massive box office disaster that it effectively destroyed United Artists studio.
Silverado (1985) was Kevin Costner's first significant western role, five years before Dances with Wolves.
Young Guns II (1990) continued the Billy the Kid story and featured a Jon Bon Jovi soundtrack including 'Blaze of Glory.'
Posse (1993), directed by Mario Van Peebles, highlighted the contributions of Black cowboys in the Old West.
One in four cowboys in the Old West was Black, but this fact was largely ignored by Hollywood until recent decades.
The Unforgiven (1960), directed by John Huston, starred Burt Lancaster and Audrey Hepburn in a western about racial prejudice — not to be confused with Eastwood's Unforgiven.
Broken Arrow (1950) was one of the first major Hollywood westerns to portray Native Americans sympathetically.
Dances with Wolves ran 181 minutes in its theatrical cut and 236 minutes in its extended edition.
Kevin Costner learned to ride bareback and speak Lakota for his role in Dances with Wolves.
The Last of the Mohicans (1992), starring Daniel Day-Lewis, incorporated western elements despite being set in colonial-era New York.
Geronimo: An American Legend (1993), directed by Walter Hill, starred Wes Studi as the legendary Apache leader.
Wes Studi became the first Native American actor to receive an Academy Honorary Award in 2019.
Dead Man's Walk (1996) and Comanche Moon (2008) were Lonesome Dove prequel miniseries based on Larry McMurtry's novels.
Into the West (2005) was a Steven Spielberg-produced miniseries following two families across 65 years of American westward expansion.
The Missing (2003), directed by Ron Howard, starred Cate Blanchett and Tommy Lee Jones in a story about rescuing a kidnapped girl in 1880s New Mexico.
Seraphim Falls (2006) starred Liam Neeson and Pierce Brosnan in a pursuit across the post-Civil War American West.
The Homesman (2014) starred Tommy Lee Jones and Hilary Swank in a bleak frontier story about transporting mentally ill women across the Nebraska Territory.
Slow West won the Grand Jury Prize at the 2015 Sundance Film Festival.
The Keeping Room (2014) was a Civil War-era western told from the perspective of three women defending their home from rogue soldiers.
Hostiles (2017) was inspired by the manuscript of a story originally written by the late screenwriter Donald E. Stewart.
First Cow (2019), directed by Kelly Reichardt, is a quiet frontier drama about the friendship between a cook and a Chinese immigrant in 1820s Oregon.
The Kid (2019) reimagined the story of Billy the Kid with Ethan Hawke as Pat Garrett and Dane DeHaan as Billy.
News of the World earned four Academy Award nominations, including for cinematography and score.
The Power of the Dog starred Benedict Cumberbatch as a menacing Montana rancher whose cruelty hides a deep secret.
Killers of the Flower Moon (2023), directed by Martin Scorsese, is based on the true story of murders of Osage Nation members in 1920s Oklahoma.
Lily Gladstone became the first Native American actress nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actress for Killers of the Flower Moon.
Killers of the Flower Moon had a budget of approximately $200 million, making it one of the most expensive westerns ever produced.
Robert De Niro played William Hale in Killers of the Flower Moon, reuniting with Leonardo DiCaprio and Martin Scorsese.
Horizon: An American Saga – Chapter 1 (2024), directed by Kevin Costner, was a passion project the actor worked on for decades.
John Wayne was awarded the Congressional Gold Medal in 1979, the highest civilian honor bestowed by Congress.
The Alamo, the 1960 John Wayne film, cost $12 million to produce — one of the most expensive films made at that time.
Gary Cooper's restrained performance in High Noon was so convincing that many audience members believed the actor was genuinely afraid.
Randolph Scott appeared in over 60 westerns during his career, becoming one of the genre's most reliable leading men.
Jimmy Stewart starred in numerous westerns, including Winchester '73 (1950), The Man from Laramie (1955), and The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance (1962).
Gregory Peck starred in several westerns, including The Gunfighter (1950), a film that influenced High Noon's real-time structure.
Burt Lancaster brought intense physicality to westerns like Gunfight at the O.K. Corral (1957) and Valdez Is Coming (1971).
Kirk Douglas starred in westerns including Gunfight at the O.K. Corral and Lonely Are the Brave (1962).
Robert Mitchum starred in El Dorado (1966) opposite John Wayne and in the Australian western The Sundowners (1960).
Yul Brynner shaved his head for The King and I and maintained the look for The Magnificent Seven, making it his signature.
Charles Bronson was born Charles Dennis Buchinsky and grew up in a coal mining town in Pennsylvania before becoming a western icon.
James Coburn was known for his laconic cool in westerns like The Magnificent Seven and Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid.
Steve McQueen's role as Vin in The Magnificent Seven established his reputation as the 'King of Cool.'
Robert Duvall has appeared in more western films and TV productions than almost any other living actor.
Tommy Lee Jones has starred in numerous westerns and played multiple real-life western figures, including Woodrow Call in Lonesome Dove.
Sam Elliott's distinctive voice and mustache have made him the quintessential modern western actor.
The western remains the only film genre to have an entire era of American history — the Old West — essentially defined by cinema rather than textbooks.
The term 'spaghetti western' was coined by Spanish journalist Alfonso Sánchez as a derogatory term that was later embraced by fans.
Django (1966) opens with the title character dragging a coffin through the mud, one of the genre's most memorable images.
The Great Silence (1968), directed by Sergio Corbucci, is a snow-set spaghetti western with one of the bleakest endings in cinema history.
Terence Hill and Bud Spencer starred in a series of popular spaghetti western comedies, including They Call Me Trinity (1970).
Franco Nero, who played the original Django, made a cameo in Tarantino's Django Unchained.
Sabata (1969) starred Lee Van Cleef as a sharpshooting bounty hunter in a lighter spaghetti western.
The spaghetti western's influence extended to Hong Kong action cinema, with John Woo citing Leone as a major inspiration.
The Italian western composers — Morricone, Bruno Nicolai, Luis Bacalov — created a distinctive musical style that blended pop, classical, and folk elements.
Luis Bacalov won the Academy Award for Best Original Score for Il Postino (1994), but his western scores for Django and others are equally celebrated.
Spaghetti westerns often featured dubbed dialogue because actors spoke their lines in different languages on set.
The western genre has inspired video games like Red Dead Redemption, which pays homage to films like The Good, the Bad and the Ugly and Unforgiven.
Red Dead Redemption 2 (2018) is considered one of the greatest video games ever made, featuring a deeply detailed western open world.
The Quick and the Dead (1995) was directed by Sam Raimi, who brought his kinetic visual style from the horror genre to a western setting.
Sharon Stone personally lobbied for Leonardo DiCaprio and Russell Crowe to be cast in The Quick and the Dead when the studio resisted.
Tombstone's production was troubled by conflicts between director Kevin Jarre and the studio, leading to a chaotic shoot.
Doc Holliday actually died of tuberculosis in a sanitarium in Glenwood Springs, Colorado, on November 8, 1887.
Wyatt Earp lived until 1929, long enough to consult with early Hollywood filmmakers about westerns.
Wyatt Earp befriended early western film stars Tom Mix and William S. Hart in his later years in Los Angeles.
The real Tombstone, Arizona, is now a tourist destination that reenacts the Gunfight at the O.K. Corral daily.
Billy the Kid's real name was likely Henry McCarty, though he also used the alias William H. Bonney.
Billy the Kid killed his first man at age 18 and was shot dead by Pat Garrett at age 21, despite legends of a longer career.
Pat Garrett was himself murdered in 1908 under mysterious circumstances that have never been fully solved.
Geronimo's name became a battle cry used by American paratroopers in World War II when jumping from planes.
Crazy Horse never allowed a photograph to be taken of him, and the famous Crazy Horse Memorial in South Dakota is based on descriptions.
The Battle of the Little Bighorn took place on June 25, 1876, and has been depicted in over 40 films.
Custer's Last Stand has been shown in films since They Died with Their Boots On (1941) starring Errol Flynn.
The transcontinental railroad, completed on May 10, 1869, effectively ended the frontier era and transformed the western landscape forever.
The Lincoln County War, a conflict between rival cattle interests in New Mexico, made Billy the Kid famous.
Deadwood, South Dakota, was a real mining camp where Wild Bill Hickok was killed and where Calamity Jane lived.
Dodge City, Kansas, was one of the roughest frontier towns and the setting for several classic western films and TV shows.
Abilene, Kansas, was the original cowtown, serving as the terminus of the Chisholm Trail and employing Wild Bill Hickok as marshal.
The Chisholm Trail, used to drive cattle from Texas to Kansas railheads, is one of the most storied routes in western mythology.
The Oregon Trail, spanning 2,170 miles from Missouri to Oregon, was traveled by over 400,000 settlers between 1840 and 1860.
Sergio Leone died on April 30, 1989, at the age of 60, before completing his planned film about the siege of Leningrad.
Ennio Morricone passed away on July 6, 2020, at the age of 91, in Rome, Italy.
John Wayne died on June 11, 1979, at the age of 72, from stomach cancer.
John Ford died on August 31, 1973, having won six Academy Awards — more than any director in history at that time.
Sam Peckinpah died on December 28, 1984, at the age of 59.
Clint Eastwood was born on May 31, 1930, in San Francisco, California.
The Academy Award for Best Picture has been won by a western film only three times: Cimarron, Dances with Wolves, and Unforgiven.
Unforgiven won Academy Awards for Best Picture, Best Director, Best Supporting Actor, and Best Film Editing.
Dances with Wolves won seven Academy Awards at the 63rd ceremony, including Best Picture and Best Director.
John Wayne was born Marion Robert Morrison on May 26, 1907, in Winterset, Iowa.
Sergio Leone was born on January 3, 1929, in Rome, the son of silent film director Vincenzo Leone.
Ennio Morricone was born on November 10, 1928, in Rome, Italy.
A Fistful of Dollars premiered in Italy on September 12, 1964.
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly premiered in Italy on December 23, 1966.
Stagecoach premiered on February 2, 1939, in a landmark moment for the western genre.
High Noon premiered on July 24, 1952.
Shane premiered on April 23, 1953.
The Searchers premiered on May 26, 1956.
